items.note: The tit-for-tat over Hormuz spread to the monarchies that host US forces. Iran launched drones at Bahrain, which its foreign ministry condemned as a sovereignty violation, and Tehran said it struck American facilities in Kuwait and Bahrain with missiles and drones. US Central Command answered with a fresh round of strikes on Iranian coastal radar and storage sites. US officials reported no major damage, and Iran's claims of hits were not independently verified, so the war widened geographically without a confirmed body count.

El Obeid stayed inside the prevention window as the Rapid Support Forces massed around the North Kordofan capital and kept up drone and air attack. UN officials warned the protective window was closing. A confirmed ground assault inside the city had not been reported, so the verified picture remained reinforcement and bombardment, with the warning still ahead of any protection.

Brent fell to roughly the low $70s before the weekend escalation landed with markets closed, leaving a Monday repricing in front of traders. Analysts tracking the lane reported the Omani-coast route going quiet, with tankers running dark and war-risk premiums sitting well above pre-war levels. The chokepoint's real price is being set by insurance and commercial behaviour more than by airstrikes.

items.what: China's model makers kept shipping competitive systems even as the US-China lab dispute sharpened, with Z.ai's latest releases drawing notice for closing the gap at lower cost. The contest is moving from who trains the best model to who can access, copy, or restrict one.

US labs faced an efficiency squeeze as buyers shifted toward cheaper inference, pressuring the spend-at-any-cost story that has underwritten the compute build-out. The repricing of AI capital expenditure now matters as much to chip names as any single model launch.

Twin powerful earthquakes struck northern Venezuela, the latest in a run of acute natural disasters competing for attention with the conflict cycle, alongside flooding from tropical storms across Japan and Taiwan.

The World Meteorological Organization put the odds of El Niño developing over June to August at roughly eighty percent, raising the risk of further weather extremes on top of a European heatwave that has already shattered June records.
